quick_glance: The holy city of Israel, capital of the Davidic kingdom and site of Solomon's Temple; central to Israelite worship, prophetic expectation, and the ministry, death, and resurrection of Jesus.
A small town south of Jerusalem, the birthplace of King David and of Jesus Christ; called 'the city of David' in the Gospels and prophesied in Micah as the origin of Israel's ruler.
A village on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, about two miles from Jerusalem; the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus, and the place where Jesus raised Lazarus from the dead.
One of the oldest cities in the world, located in the Jordan Valley near the Dead Sea; famous for its conquest by Joshua when its walls fell, and visited by Jesus who healed blind Bartimaeus there.
An ancient city in the hill country of Judah where Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob were buried in the Cave of Machpelah; later David's first capital before he moved to Jerusalem.
A village about seven miles from Jerusalem where the risen Jesus appeared to two disciples on the road, breaking bread with them before vanishing from their sight.
The elevated platform in Jerusalem where Solomon built the First Temple and where the Second Temple stood during the time of Jesus; considered the holiest site in Judaism.
The oldest settled part of Jerusalem, captured by David from the Jebusites and made the capital of his united kingdom; located on the southeastern ridge south of the Temple Mount.
A ridge of hills east of Jerusalem separated from the city by the Kidron Valley; the site of Jesus' agony in Gethsemane, his prophetic discourse, and his ascension into heaven.
A garden at the foot of the Mount of Olives where Jesus prayed in agony on the night before his crucifixion and where he was arrested by the temple guards.
The site outside the walls of Jerusalem where Jesus was crucified; the name means 'place of a skull' in Aramaic.
A rock-cut pool at the southern end of the City of David fed by Hezekiah's tunnel from the Gihon Spring; the place where Jesus sent a blind man to wash and receive his sight.
A pool in Jerusalem near the Sheep Gate with five covered colonnades, where Jesus healed a man who had been an invalid for thirty-eight years.
A Roman military fortress at the northwest corner of the Temple Mount in Jerusalem, built by Herod the Great; the likely site of Jesus' trial before Pontius Pilate.
A valley separating Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives to the east; David crossed it weeping when fleeing Absalom, and Jesus crossed it on the night of his arrest.
A valley south and west of Jerusalem associated with child sacrifice to Molech; later used as a metaphor for the fires of divine judgment and the concept of hell.
A small village on the Mount of Olives near Bethany, from which Jesus sent two disciples to bring the donkey for his triumphal entry into Jerusalem.
An oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea known for its fresh-water springs and vineyards; the place where David hid from Saul in caves.
An archaeological site on the northwestern shore of the Dead Sea where the Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered in nearby caves; associated with an ancient Jewish sectarian community.
A town in the hill country of Judah about ten miles south of Jerusalem; the home of the prophet Amos and the wise woman sent by Joab to persuade David.
A Levitical city in the territory of Benjamin, about three miles northeast of Jerusalem; the birthplace and home of the prophet Jeremiah.
A town in the territory of Benjamin that served as King Saul's royal residence; also the site of the infamous outrage described in Judges 19 that led to civil war.
A Hivite city northwest of Jerusalem whose inhabitants deceived Joshua into making a treaty with them; later the site of Solomon's dream at the great high place.
The ancient designation of Bethlehem distinguishing it from another Bethlehem in Zebulun; the prophetic name used by Micah to identify the birthplace of the coming ruler of Israel.
disambiguation_note: Alternate designation for Bethlehem in Judah, as used in Micah 5:2

quick_glance: A furnished room in Jerusalem where Jesus shared the Last Supper with his disciples and where the Holy Spirit descended on the believers at Pentecost.
A small village in lower Galilee where Jesus grew up and from which he derived the title 'Jesus of Nazareth'; the place where the angel Gabriel announced the birth of Christ to Mary.
A prosperous fishing town on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee that served as the base of Jesus' Galilean ministry; the home of Peter and the site of many healings and teachings.
A fishing village on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee; the hometown of the apostles Peter, Andrew, and Philip, and the area where Jesus fed the five thousand.
A town on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee known for its fish-salting industry; the hometown of Mary Magdalene, one of the most prominent female followers of Jesus.
A village in Galilee where Jesus performed his first miracle, turning water into wine at a wedding feast; also the home of the apostle Nathanael.
A small town in Galilee south of Mount Tabor where Jesus raised a widow's only son from the dead as the funeral procession was leaving the town.
A town north of the Sea of Galilee denounced by Jesus along with Bethsaida and Capernaum for failing to repent despite the mighty works performed there.
A city on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee built by Herod Antipas and named in honor of the Roman emperor Tiberius; it gave its name to the sea and later became a center of Jewish learning.

The administrative capital of Galilee during Jesus' youth, located about four miles northwest of Nazareth; a major Hellenistic city that some scholars believe Joseph and Jesus may have worked on as craftsmen.
A city at the base of Mount Hermon in northern Galilee, rebuilt by Herod's son Philip; the place where Peter confessed Jesus as the Christ and Jesus foretold his death.

A prominent dome-shaped mountain in the Jezreel Valley, traditionally identified as the site of the Transfiguration of Jesus; also the place where Deborah and Barak gathered their army against Sisera.
A mountain ridge extending to the Mediterranean coast where the prophet Elijah challenged and defeated the 450 prophets of Baal, calling down fire from heaven.

The highest mountain in the region at over 9,000 feet, marking the northern boundary of Israelite territory; its snow-capped peak was visible from much of the land and possibly the site of the Transfiguration.
A large freshwater lake in northern Israel around which much of Jesus' ministry took place, including the calling of fishermen-disciples, the calming of the storm, and walking on water.
A large fertile valley in northern Israel stretching from Mount Carmel to the Jordan; the site of many decisive battles including those of Gideon, Saul, and the prophesied battle of Armageddon.
A strategically important fortified city overlooking the Jezreel Valley, controlling a major trade route; Solomon made it a chariot city, and its name gave rise to 'Armageddon' in Revelation.
An ancient Canaanite city near Megiddo in the Jezreel Valley; mentioned in the Song of Deborah as the site where Israelite forces defeated the Canaanite kings.
A village near Mount Tabor where Saul consulted a medium on the eve of his final battle against the Philistines at Gilboa, and the spirit of Samuel was called up.
A village in the territory of Issachar near the Jezreel Valley; the home of the Shunammite woman who hosted the prophet Elisha and whose son he raised from the dead.
A city in the territory of Issachar where the kings of Israel had a royal residence; the site of Naboth's vineyard seized by Ahab and Jezebel, and where Jezebel met her death.
disambiguation_note: The city, not the valley

quick_glance: The capital city of the northern kingdom of Israel, built by King Omri on a hill; later destroyed by Assyria in 722 BC and rebuilt by Herod the Great as Sebaste.
disambiguation_note: The city, not the region

quick_glance: An ancient city between Mount Gerizim and Mount Ebal; the place where God first promised the land to Abraham, where Jacob built an altar, and where the covenant was renewed under Joshua.
A village in Samaria near Jacob's well where Jesus spoke with the Samaritan woman, offering her 'living water' and revealing himself as the Messiah.
The mountain near Shechem designated as the mount of blessing where six tribes pronounced blessings upon Israel; later the site of the Samaritan temple and a center of Samaritan worship.
The mountain opposite Mount Gerizim near Shechem where the curses of the law were pronounced; Joshua built an altar there and wrote the law of Moses on stones.
A beautiful Canaanite city that served as the first capital of the northern kingdom of Israel before Omri moved the capital to Samaria; praised for beauty in Song of Solomon.
A town in the hill country of Samaria where Joseph found his brothers pasturing their flocks and was sold into slavery; also where Elisha's servant saw the heavenly army of chariots of fire.
The central sanctuary of Israel during the period of the judges, where the tabernacle and the Ark of the Covenant were kept; the place where the boy Samuel heard God's voice.
A city north of Jerusalem where Jacob dreamed of a ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending; later a major shrine where Jeroboam set up one of his golden calves.
A strategic city on the Via Maris trade route in the Plain of Sharon where the Philistines camped before capturing the Ark of the Covenant from Israel in the days of Eli.
disambiguation_note: The Aphek near Samaria, not the one in the Golan

quick_glance: A magnificent port city built by Herod the Great on the Mediterranean coast; the seat of the Roman governor of Judea, where Paul was imprisoned for two years and from which he sailed to Rome.
disambiguation_note: The coastal city, not Caesarea Philippi

quick_glance: An ancient Mediterranean port city where the prophet Jonah boarded a ship to flee from God's command; also the place where Peter raised Dorcas from the dead and received the vision of the sheet.
One of the five principal cities of the Philistines on the Mediterranean coast; mentioned in Samson's exploits and in David's lament over Saul and Jonathan.
One of the five chief cities of the Philistines where the captured Ark of the Covenant was placed in the temple of Dagon, causing the idol to fall; later visited by Philip the evangelist.
The southernmost of the five major Philistine cities, near the border of Egypt; where Samson was imprisoned and pulled down the temple of Dagon on himself and the Philistines.
One of the five chief cities of the Philistines and the home of the giant Goliath whom David slew; also the city to which David fled for refuge under King Achish.
The northernmost of the five chief Philistine cities; the place to which the Ark was sent before it was returned to Israel, and site of the idol Baal-zebub consulted by Ahaziah.
A heavily fortified Judahite city in the Shephelah that was the second most important city after Jerusalem; besieged by Sennacherib of Assyria and later by Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon.
The Hellenistic and Roman name for the Philistine city of Ashdod; where Philip the evangelist found himself after baptizing the Ethiopian eunuch on the road to Gaza.
disambiguation_note: Hellenistic name for Ashdod

quick_glance: A port city on the northern coast of Israel, known as Acco in the Old Testament; Paul visited the believers there for one day during his voyage to Jerusalem.
A great Phoenician port city famous for its commerce and purple dye; allied with Solomon in building the Temple, and later denounced by the prophets for its pride and luxury.
An ancient Phoenician city north of Tyre, one of the oldest cities in the region; the home of Jezebel and a place Jesus visited during his ministry in the region of Tyre and Sidon.
A Phoenician town between Tyre and Sidon where Elijah was sustained by a poor widow whose oil and flour did not run out; Jesus cited this event in the synagogue at Nazareth.
An ancient port city on the northern coast of Israel that the tribe of Asher failed to conquer; later known as Ptolemais in the Hellenistic period and Acre during the Crusades.
disambiguation_note: OT name for Ptolemais/Acre

quick_glance: A Canaanite and later Israelite port city on the Mediterranean coast south of Mount Carmel; included in one of Solomon's administrative districts.
A major Greco-Roman city of the Decapolis in the Transjordan; the nearby region is identified with the 'country of the Gerasenes' where Jesus cast demons into a herd of swine.
A fortified city of the Decapolis east of the Sea of Galilee; associated with the healing of the Gadarene demoniacs, one of whom Legion was cast out of by Jesus.
A Decapolis city in the Transjordan to which early Christians fled from Jerusalem before its destruction in AD 70, according to the church historian Eusebius.
The only Decapolis city west of the Jordan River, previously known as Beth-shean; where the Philistines hung the bodies of Saul and his sons on the city walls.
A Greco-Roman city of the Decapolis perched on a hill east of the Sea of Galilee; sometimes identified as the 'city set on a hill' that 'cannot be hidden' in Jesus' Sermon on the Mount.
A town in Gilead east of the Jordan where Jacob saw angels of God and named the place 'two camps'; later the temporary capital of Ish-bosheth and a refuge for David during Absalom's revolt.
A place east of the Jordan River where Jacob wrestled with God until daybreak and received the name Israel; Jacob called the place Peniel, meaning 'face of God.'
A river in the Transjordan flowing westward into the Jordan River; the place where Jacob wrestled with God on the eve of his reunion with Esau.
A mountainous region east of the Jordan River known for its forests, pastures, and healing balm; the home of the prophet Elijah the Tishbite and the judge Jephthah.
The territory east of the Jordan occupied by the descendants of Lot's son Ben-ammi; its capital was Rabbah, where David's general Joab defeated the Ammonites.
A territory east of the Dead Sea, home of the Moabites descended from Lot; the setting of the book of Ruth and the place where Moses died on Mount Nebo overlooking the Promised Land.
A mountainous territory south of the Dead Sea occupied by the descendants of Esau; the Edomites were perpetual rivals of Israel and the subject of Obadiah's prophecy.
The rock-cut city in the mountains of Edom, possibly the 'Sela' of the Old Testament; a major Nabataean trading center carved into red sandstone cliffs.
An ancient capital city of Edom, prominent in prophetic oracles of judgment; Isaiah's vivid vision describes the Lord coming from Bozrah with garments stained crimson.
An oasis in the wilderness south of Canaan where the Israelites camped for an extended period during the wilderness wandering; the place from which the twelve spies were sent into the Promised Land.
A city in the Negev marking the traditional southern boundary of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba'); where Abraham made a covenant with Abimelech and Hagar wandered with Ishmael.
A Canaanite city in the Negev whose king attacked the Israelites during the wilderness period; later an Israelite fortress with a temple that included an altar of burnt offerings.
A Canaanite city in the Negev destroyed by Israel during the conquest; its name means 'destruction,' commemorating the devotion of the city to destruction.
A Moabite city east of the Dead Sea where the famous Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele) was discovered; occupied at various times by the Israelites, especially the tribe of Gad.
A Moabite city on the plateau east of the Dead Sea; a territory contested between Israel and Moab, mentioned in the oracle against Moab in Isaiah.

The principal river of Israel flowing from Mount Hermon south through the Sea of Galilee to the Dead Sea; the river the Israelites crossed to enter the Promised Land and where Jesus was baptized by John.
A hypersaline lake at the lowest point on earth, at the southern end of the Jordan Valley; the region where Sodom and Gomorrah were destroyed and near where the Dead Sea Scrolls were found.
The flat area east of the Jordan River opposite Jericho where the Israelites camped before crossing into the Promised Land; the setting of Moses' final addresses in Deuteronomy.
A river flowing westward into the Dead Sea that formed the boundary between Moab and the Amorite territory conquered by Israel; a deep gorge that served as a natural defense.
A seasonal stream flowing into the southern end of the Dead Sea, marking the boundary between Edom and Moab; the Israelites crossed it during the wilderness wandering.
The barren desert region east of the Judean hills descending to the Dead Sea; where David hid from Saul, John the Baptist preached, and Jesus was tempted by Satan for forty days.
The arid region forming the southern part of the land of Israel; Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob sojourned there, and it served as the southern boundary of Israelite settlement.
The great rift valley extending from the Sea of Galilee through the Dead Sea to the Gulf of Aqaba; a geographical feature forming the Jordan Valley and eastern boundary of the land.
The Old Testament name for the Dead Sea, the extremely salty body of water at the lowest elevation on earth; it marked the eastern boundary of the tribe of Judah.
disambiguation_note: OT designation for the Dead Sea

quick_glance: The fertile oasis surrounding the city of Jericho in the Jordan Valley, watered by springs; where the Israelites first camped after crossing the Jordan into the Promised Land.
An ancient Sumerian city in southern Mesopotamia from which God called Abraham to leave for the Promised Land; one of the most important cities of early civilization.
A city in upper Mesopotamia where Abraham's father Terah settled and died; Abraham sojourned there before journeying to Canaan, and Jacob later fled there to his uncle Laban.

One of the cities of the plain near the Dead Sea destroyed by God with fire and brimstone because of its great wickedness; Abraham had pleaded with God to spare it if even ten righteous could be found.
One of the cities of the plain destroyed together with Sodom by divine judgment; frequently cited alongside Sodom as a byword for extreme sinfulness and divine punishment.
A small city near Sodom to which Lot fled when Sodom and Gomorrah were destroyed; spared from destruction because of Lot's request, its name means 'small.'
A site near Hebron where Abraham settled and built an altar to the Lord; under the oaks of Mamre, Abraham entertained three visitors who announced that Sarah would bear a son.
The well dug by Abraham's servants and the site of his covenant with Abimelech; the name Beersheba means 'well of the oath' or 'well of seven.'
disambiguation_note: The well itself, not the city

quick_glance: A place in the Transjordan where Jacob and Laban made a covenant and set up a stone pillar as a witness between them; Laban said 'The LORD watch between me and you.'
disambiguation_note: Mizpah in Gilead, not in Benjamin

quick_glance: A town in the territory of Benjamin north of Jerusalem; associated with Rachel's weeping for her children and the home of the prophet Samuel's parents.
disambiguation_note: Ramah in Benjamin, not Ramah of Ephraim

quick_glance: A strategically located Canaanite city on the border between the hill country and the coastal plain; given by Pharaoh to Solomon as a wedding gift when he married Pharaoh's daughter.

A city at the northernmost extent of Israel, originally called Laish; captured by the tribe of Dan and made a center of idolatrous worship with a graven image, and later one of Jeroboam's golden calves.
disambiguation_note: The city, not the tribe

quick_glance: The largest and most important Canaanite city in northern Israel, whose king Jabin led a coalition against Joshua; later rebuilt by Solomon as a major fortified city.
An alternate name for Peniel, the place where Jacob wrestled with God; Gideon later destroyed its tower because the men of the city refused to help feed his army.
disambiguation_note: Alternate spelling of Peniel

quick_glance: A town in the Jordan Valley east of the river where Jacob built booths for his livestock after returning from Haran; also the place where Gideon punished the elders who refused aid.
disambiguation_note: Succoth in the Jordan Valley

quick_glance: A city near the junction of the Jabbok and Jordan rivers where the waters of the Jordan were cut off, allowing the Israelites to cross into the Promised Land on dry ground.
disambiguation_note: The city in the Jordan Valley, not the first man

quick_glance: The mountain in the wilderness where God gave the Ten Commandments to Moses amid thunder, lightning, and a thick cloud; also where Elijah fled and heard God's still small voice.
The triangular peninsula between Egypt and Canaan where the Israelites wandered for forty years after the Exodus; the setting of most events from Exodus through Deuteronomy.
The wilderness region around Kadesh Barnea where the Israelites spent much of their forty years of wandering after the failed attempt to enter the Promised Land.
disambiguation_note: The wilderness area, not the settlement

quick_glance: A desert region in the northern Sinai adjacent to Kadesh Barnea; where Miriam died and Moses struck the rock to bring forth water, an act of disobedience that barred him from the Promised Land.
A large desert in the northeastern Sinai Peninsula where Ishmael grew up and the Israelites camped; the starting point for the twelve spies sent to explore the land of Canaan.
A desert region east of Egypt through which the Israelites traveled after crossing the Red Sea; also where Hagar fled from Sarah and was met by the angel of the Lord.
A desert region between Elim and Sinai where the Israelites grumbled for food and God provided manna and quail for the first time.
disambiguation_note: Not to be confused with the Wilderness of Zin

quick_glance: A fertile region in the eastern Nile Delta where Jacob's family settled under Joseph's patronage; the Israelites multiplied there during their centuries in Egypt before the Exodus.
disambiguation_note: The region in Egypt, not any Canaan location

quick_glance: An Egyptian city in the eastern Delta built by Israelite slave labor; the departure point of the Exodus when the Israelites left Egypt after the final plague.
One of the Egyptian store cities built by Israelite slave labor before the Exodus; mentioned alongside Raamses as a product of Pharaoh's oppression of the Hebrews.

The northern region of Israel, a fertile and populous area around the Sea of Galilee; the primary setting of Jesus' ministry, where he called his disciples, taught, and performed most of his miracles.
disambiguation_note: The region, not the sea

quick_glance: The southern region of Israel centered on Jerusalem, the heartland of the tribe of Judah; the territory of the kingdom of Judah and later the Roman province where Jesus was crucified and rose again.
disambiguation_note: The region, not the tribe

quick_glance: The central hill country between Galilee and Judea, inhabited by the Samaritans in New Testament times; a region largely avoided by Jews but traversed by Jesus, who ministered to Samaritans.
disambiguation_note: The region, not the city

quick_glance: The territory east of the Jordan River under Herod Antipas; where John the Baptist ministered and where Jesus taught during his later Judean ministry.
disambiguation_note: The region, not a specific city

quick_glance: The Hellenistic and Roman name for the territory of the Edomites, which had shifted northward into southern Judea; the homeland of the Herodian dynasty, as Herod the Great was Idumean.
disambiguation_note: NT-era designation for the region of Edom

quick_glance: The narrow coastal strip north of Israel including the great port cities of Tyre and Sidon; famous for seafaring, trade, purple dye, and the Phoenician alphabet.
A large territory to the northeast of Israel with its capital at Damascus; the Arameans were frequent opponents of Israel, and Syrian Antioch became the base for Paul's missionary journeys.
The collective term for the territories east of the Jordan River, including Gilead, Bashan, Ammon, Moab, and Edom; allotted to the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh.
The low rolling hills between the Judean highlands and the coastal plain of Philistia; a contested border region and the setting of many conflicts between Israel and the Philistines.
A league of ten Greco-Roman cities mostly east of the Jordan River; large crowds from the Decapolis followed Jesus, and it was the region where the healed demoniac proclaimed what Jesus had done.
The ancient civilization of the Nile valley; the land of Israel's enslavement from which God delivered them in the Exodus, and a place of refuge for the infant Jesus.
disambiguation_note: The land/country as a place

quick_glance: The magnificent capital of the Babylonian Empire on the Euphrates River, famed for its hanging gardens and massive walls; the city to which the Jews were exiled in 586 BC.
disambiguation_note: The city, not the empire

quick_glance: The great capital of the Assyrian Empire on the Tigris River, called 'that great city'; the city to which God sent the reluctant prophet Jonah to preach repentance.
The territory in northern Mesopotamia centered on the cities of Assur, Nineveh, and Calah; the empire that conquered the northern kingdom of Israel in 722 BC.
disambiguation_note: The region/land, not the empire as a political entity

quick_glance: The territory east of Mesopotamia whose empire under Cyrus the Great conquered Babylon and permitted the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple.
disambiguation_note: The land, not the empire

quick_glance: The region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, the cradle of civilization; the homeland of Abraham before God called him, and the location of Babylon and Nineveh.
The specific biblical designation for Ur emphasizing its location in the land of the Chaldeans; the ancestral city from which God called Abraham to go to a land he would show him.
disambiguation_note: Full biblical name for Ur

quick_glance: The ancient capital of Lower Egypt at the apex of the Nile Delta; a great center of Egyptian worship and power, denounced by the prophets Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Hosea.
The great capital of Upper Egypt, called No or No-amon in the Bible; the prophet Nahum cited its fall as a warning to Nineveh despite its mighty defenses.
The great Hellenistic city in Egypt founded by Alexander the Great; home to a large Jewish community, the Septuagint translation of the Old Testament, and Apollos the eloquent preacher.
One of the two great rivers of Mesopotamia, forming the northern boundary of the Promised Land as described to Abraham; mentioned from Genesis to Revelation.
One of the two great rivers of Mesopotamia, flowing through Assyria past Nineveh; identified in Genesis as the third river flowing from the Garden of Eden.
The great river of Egypt whose annual flooding sustained the civilization of the Nile Valley; God turned it to blood in the first plague, and Moses was placed in a basket among its reeds.

The mountain or mountain range in the region of Armenia where Noah's ark came to rest after the flood waters receded; its exact location remains debated but traditionally identified in eastern Turkey.
A territory in what is now northwestern Iran whose people were allied with the Persians; 'the law of the Medes and Persians' was proverbially unalterable, as seen in the book of Daniel.
An ancient territory east of Mesopotamia whose capital was Susa; the setting of the events of the book of Esther and the location of Daniel's vision by the Ulai canal.
A strategic city on the Euphrates in northern Syria where Nebuchadnezzar defeated Pharaoh Necho in 605 BC, a turning-point battle that established Babylonian supremacy in the ancient Near East.
An ancient city on the Euphrates in eastern Syria, a major trading hub of the ancient world; although not mentioned in the Bible, its extensive archives illuminate the patriarchal period.
An ancient port city on the Syrian coast whose discovery provided Canaanite religious texts that greatly illuminated the cultural background of the Old Testament; destroyed around 1185 BC.
An alternate spelling of Haran, the Mesopotamian city where Abraham's family settled; an important trading center at the crossroads of major ancient trade routes.
disambiguation_note: Alternate spelling of Haran

quick_glance: The capital of the Roman Empire and the city to which Paul traveled as a prisoner; the destination of his epistle to the Romans and the traditional site of his martyrdom.

A wealthy and cosmopolitan Greek city on the isthmus connecting mainland Greece to the Peloponnese; Paul spent eighteen months there founding the church and later wrote two epistles to the Corinthians.
A major city of Asia Minor and center of the cult of Artemis; Paul ministered there for about three years, and it later received one of the seven letters in Revelation.
The third-largest city of the Roman Empire, where believers were first called Christians; the home church of Paul and Barnabas and the launching point for Paul's missionary journeys.
disambiguation_note: Antioch in Syria, not Antioch of Pisidia

quick_glance: A Roman colony in the province of Galatia in central Asia Minor; Paul preached in its synagogue on his first missionary journey, provoking both great interest and fierce opposition.
disambiguation_note: Antioch in Pisidia, not Syrian Antioch

quick_glance: A Roman colony in Macedonia where Paul established the first Christian church in Europe; the place where Paul and Silas were imprisoned and the jailer was converted.
The capital of the Roman province of Macedonia where Paul founded a church despite being driven out by persecution; he wrote two epistles to encourage the believers there.
A Macedonian city whose Jewish community was praised as 'more noble' than those in Thessalonica because they examined the Scriptures daily to verify Paul's teaching.
The cultural and intellectual capital of the ancient world where Paul preached on the Areopagus about the 'unknown god'; a center of Greek philosophy, art, and learning.
An island in the central Mediterranean where Paul was shipwrecked on his voyage to Rome; the islanders showed unusual kindness, and Paul healed many sick people during his three-month stay.
A port city on the Bay of Naples in Italy where Paul landed on his voyage to Rome and found Christian brothers who hosted him for seven days.
A city in Lycaonia in central Asia Minor where Paul healed a crippled man and was mistaken for Hermes; the hometown of Timothy, Paul's trusted companion.
A city in the Lycaonian region of Galatia where Paul preached and won many disciples; the hometown of Gaius, one of Paul's traveling companions.
A city in central Asia Minor where Paul and Barnabas preached in the synagogue on the first missionary journey but were forced to flee when Jewish and Gentile opponents plotted to stone them.
A city in the Lycus Valley of Asia Minor to whose church Paul wrote the epistle to the Colossians, warning against syncretistic heresy; Paul himself may never have visited the city.
A wealthy city in the Lycus Valley of Asia Minor, one of the seven churches addressed in Revelation; rebuked by Christ for being 'lukewarm, neither hot nor cold.'
A prosperous port city on the Aegean coast of Asia Minor; one of the seven churches in Revelation, praised for enduring suffering and told to 'be faithful unto death.'
A major city of Asia Minor known for its great library and its altar of Zeus; one of the seven churches in Revelation, described as dwelling 'where Satan's throne is.'
A city in western Asia Minor known for its purple dye industry; one of the seven churches in Revelation, and the hometown of Lydia, Paul's first convert in Europe.
An ancient and wealthy city of Asia Minor, once the capital of the kingdom of Lydia; one of the seven churches in Revelation, warned that it had a reputation for being alive but was dead.
A city in western Asia Minor, one of the seven churches in Revelation; commended for keeping Christ's word and promised an 'open door' that no one could shut.
disambiguation_note: The city in Asia Minor, not modern Philadelphia

quick_glance: A port city in northwestern Asia Minor where Paul received the vision of a man from Macedonia calling for help; also where the young man Eutychus fell from a window during Paul's long sermon.
An ancient port city on the coast of Asia Minor where Paul met with the elders of the Ephesian church for a moving farewell address on his final journey to Jerusalem.
A port city on the eastern coast of Cyprus where Paul and Barnabas began their first missionary journey by preaching in the Jewish synagogues.
A city on the western coast of Cyprus where Paul confronted the sorcerer Bar-Jesus and the Roman proconsul Sergius Paulus became a believer.
A city on the southern coast of Lycia in Asia Minor where Paul changed ships during his voyage to Rome as a prisoner.
A city on the southwestern tip of Asia Minor past which Paul's ship sailed with difficulty on the voyage to Rome because of contrary winds.
A port city on the southern tip of Italy where Paul's ship stopped briefly on the final leg of his journey to Rome.
A city in Macedonia through which Paul and Silas passed on their way from Philippi to Thessalonica along the Egnatian Way.
A city in Macedonia on the Via Egnatia through which Paul and Silas passed traveling between Amphipolis and Thessalonica.
One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, capital of Aram/Syria; the place where Paul was traveling when he encountered the risen Jesus and was struck blind.
The capital of the Roman province of Cilicia and the birthplace of the apostle Paul, who described it as 'no insignificant city'; a center of learning and Hellenistic culture.
The seaport of Antioch in Syria from which Paul and Barnabas set sail on the first missionary journey to Cyprus.
The same coastal city of Caesarea Maritima viewed as a significant location in Paul's journeys; where Paul was held prisoner for two years under Felix and Festus before appealing to Caesar.
disambiguation_note: Caesarea Maritima as it relates to Paul's imprisonment

quick_glance: A Roman province in northwestern Asia Minor where Paul and his companions were prevented by the Spirit from preaching; later the destination of Peter's first epistle.
A Roman province on the southern shore of the Black Sea in northern Asia Minor; Jews from Pontus were in Jerusalem at Pentecost, and it was one of the regions addressed in First Peter.
A large Roman province in central Asia Minor; Jews from Cappadocia were in Jerusalem at Pentecost, and it was one of the regions addressed in First Peter.
A Roman province in central Asia Minor to whose churches Paul wrote the epistle to the Galatians, defending justification by faith rather than works of the law.
The Roman province comprising most of southern Greece with its capital at Corinth; Paul spent considerable time there, and Gallio the proconsul dismissed the charges against him.
A Roman province in northern Greece where Paul established churches at Philippi, Thessalonica, and Berea after receiving the Macedonian call in a vision at Troas.
The large peninsula (modern Turkey) comprising numerous Roman provinces; the setting of Paul's missionary journeys and the location of the seven churches addressed in Revelation.
disambiguation_note: The broader region, not the Roman province of 'Asia'

quick_glance: The desert region south and east of Israel; Paul retreated to Arabia after his conversion before returning to Damascus, and the term broadly includes the Nabataean kingdom.
A duplicate reference for the Pisidian Antioch where Paul's major synagogue sermon is recorded in Acts 13, resulting in widespread Gentile interest and Jewish opposition.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for Antioch of Pisidia in the NT narrative

quick_glance: A small Roman province on the southern coast of Asia Minor; Paul passed through its port cities of Myra and Patara on his sea voyages.
A small Roman province on the southern coast of Asia Minor; Paul passed through its chief city Perga, where John Mark left the missionary party and returned to Jerusalem.
A small rocky island in the Aegean Sea where the apostle John was exiled 'on account of the word of God' and received the visions recorded in the book of Revelation.
A large island in the eastern Mediterranean and the homeland of Barnabas; the first destination of Paul's first missionary journey, where they traveled from Salamis to Paphos.
A large Greek island in the Mediterranean Sea; Paul's ship passed along its coast on the voyage to Rome, and Titus was left there to appoint elders in every town.
A large Greek island off the southwestern coast of Asia Minor; Paul's ship stopped there on his return voyage to Jerusalem.
A small island south of Crete under the lee of which Paul's ship briefly found shelter during the violent storm on the voyage to Rome.
An alternate reference for the great Canaanite city of Hazor in upper Galilee, the largest tell in Israel; its king headed the northern coalition against Joshua during the conquest.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Hazor

quick_glance: An alternate reference for the fortified city of Gezer on the western approach to Jerusalem; one of Solomon's three chariot cities along with Hazor and Megiddo.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Gezer

quick_glance: A fortified outpost in the Negev desert built or rebuilt by Solomon to protect the southern trade routes; possibly the same as Tadmor (Palmyra) in the wilderness.
An alternate reference for the Canaanite city of Arad whose king fought against Israel in the wilderness; an important fortress in the Negev region.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Arad

quick_glance: A Canaanite city in the hill country of Judah captured by Othniel, who was given Caleb's daughter Achsah as a reward; it was formerly known as Kiriath-sepher, meaning 'city of books.'
A Canaanite city in the Shephelah conquered by Joshua; it later revolted against Judah during the reign of Jehoram and was besieged by Sennacherib of Assyria.
A fortified city in the lowlands of Judah where King Asa defeated the Ethiopian army of Zerah with a million troops; later an important Idumean city in the Hellenistic period.
A town in the Valley of Elah in the Shephelah of Judah near where the Philistines camped before David's battle with Goliath.
A Canaanite city in the Shephelah near a cave where David hid from Saul and gathered around himself a band of about four hundred discontented men.
A town in the territory of Benjamin that served as a gathering place for Israel; Samuel judged Israel there, and it became the seat of Gedaliah's government after the fall of Jerusalem.
disambiguation_note: Mizpah in Benjamin, not in Gilead

quick_glance: An alternate reference for the important city of Gibeon, site of the great high place where Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings and God appeared to him in a dream.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Gibeon

quick_glance: A priestly city near Jerusalem where David fled from Saul and was given the holy bread and Goliath's sword by Ahimelech the priest; Saul later massacred the priests of Nob.
A town in the hill country of Ephraim, the birthplace and home of the prophet Samuel; sometimes identified with Arimathea of the New Testament.
disambiguation_note: Ramah in Ephraim, not in Benjamin

quick_glance: An alternate reference for the city of Tirzah that was praised for its beauty and served as the capital of the northern kingdom before Omri built Samaria.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Tirzah

quick_glance: A city in the Transjordan whose inhabitants were rescued by Saul from the Ammonites in his first military victory as king; its people later honored Saul by recovering his body from Beth-shean.
A Levitical city of refuge in the Transjordan; the site of the battle where King Ahab of Israel was killed by a random arrow despite his disguise.
A city in Bashan where Moses defeated Og, the last of the giant Rephaim kings, in battle; later allotted to the half-tribe of Manasseh east of the Jordan.
A fertile region east of the Sea of Galilee known for its oak forests, rich pastures, and well-fed cattle; ruled by the giant King Og before being conquered by Moses.
The capital city of the Ammonites, modern-day Amman; where Uriah the Hittite was killed on David's orders during the siege, and where Og's iron bedstead was preserved.
The capital of Moab, a fortified city that withstood siege by Israel, Judah, and Edom until the king of Moab sacrificed his firstborn son on the wall.
A city on the northern edge of the Arnon Gorge, marking the southern boundary of the territory conquered by Israel east of the Jordan; allotted to the tribe of Reuben.
An alternate reference for the Moabite city of Dibon, the site where the Mesha Stele was discovered in 1868, providing extrabiblical confirmation of the Moabite conflict with Israel.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Dibon

quick_glance: A mountain in Moab from which Moses viewed the Promised Land before his death; the peak is traditionally identified with Jebel en-Neba, offering a panoramic view of Canaan.
disambiguation_note: Mount Nebo, not the Babylonian god

quick_glance: An alternate reference for the town of Mahanaim in Gilead; designated as a Levitical city and a city of refuge for those east of the Jordan.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for the city of Mahanaim

quick_glance: An alternate reference for the town of Succoth in the Jordan Valley; its association with the casting of bronze vessels for Solomon's Temple highlights its metalworking industry.
disambiguation_note: Alternate reference for Succoth in the Jordan Valley

quick_glance: A town in Gilead in the Transjordan where Mephibosheth, the crippled son of Jonathan, lived in the house of Machir until David restored him to the royal table.
A place in the Transjordan where David defeated the Aramean forces under Hadadezer of Zobah, who had brought reinforcements from beyond the Euphrates.
